---
title: "Star Wars Survey Analysis"
subtitle: "Unit 5 Task 3"
author: "Vincent Gentry"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

```{python}
import polars as pl 
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from lets_plot import *
import polars.selectors as cs
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, classification_report

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)

data_url = "https://byuidatascience.github.io/DS250-Course-Polars/Data/StarWars_with_emails.csv"
```

<!-- The first 10 rows are not part of the datset we need.

The columns headers are split across two rows. We need to clean and simplify the column headers-->

```{python}
# Read in and tidy up the first row
a = pl.Series(pl.read_csv(data_url, skip_rows = 10, n_rows=1, has_header=False, encoding = 'utf8-lossy').row(0))
# Note the non-aschii character in the column "Do you consider yourself to be a fan of the Expanded Universe". (column AF if you open it in Excel). 

# BTW: Detecting what it is and where can be difficult

# Lossy means that invalid utf8 values are replaced with \uFFFD characters. When using other encodings than utf8 or utf8-lossy, the input is first decoded in memory with python. Defaults to utf8...ignore_errors = True does not replace the non-utf8 character with a blank space like you would hope

a[2] = "seen_any" #simply overwrite the value at index 2

# Rather than changing each column header manually,
# here we use regex to apply changes more efficiently
a = (
  a.str.replace(r".+seen.+", "seen_")
   .str.replace(r".+rank.+", "rank_")
   .str.replace(r"(.+favorably.+)", "fav_")
   .fill_null(strategy = "forward")

   .str.replace("\uFFFD+", "") #this gets rid of the question marks in a diamond characters 
   .str.strip_chars()
   .str.to_lowercase()
   .str.replace_all(" ", "_")
   .str.replace(r"location(.+)", "location")

)
a[3] = "star_wars_fan"
a[30] = "who_shot_first"
a[31] = "know_expanded_universe"
a[32] = "expanded_universe_fan"
a[33] = "star_trek_fan"
```

<!-- Combine series q and a into one series to be used as column names-->

```{python}
b = pl.Series(pl.read_csv(data_url, skip_rows = 11, n_rows=1, has_header=False, encoding = 'utf8-lossy').row(0))

b = (
  b.str.replace(r"Star Wars: (Episode \w+) .+", "${1}")
   .str.replace("Response", "")
   .str.replace_all(" ", "_")
   .fill_null("")
   .str.strip_chars()
)

```

```{python}
#Now combine the two series, each of which contains part of the desired column header
new_names = (a + b).to_list()

# Read in the data, skipping over the column header rows
# and use 'new_names' as the column headers
df = pl.read_csv(data_url, skip_rows=12, has_header=False, new_columns = new_names )
```

## Executive Summary

- **Goal:** The goal of this project is to clean up the FiveThirtyEight Star Wars survey data, verify it matches the original article, and build a machine learning model that predicts whether someone makes at least $50k a year.
- **Data:** About 1,186 people were surveyed in 2014 about Star Wars. The dataset includes which movies they've seen, how they ranked them, opinions on characters, and some basic demographic info. Fake names and emails were added to the dataset for this project.
- **Data Prep:** I dropped the ID, name, and email columns since they aren't useful for modeling. I created a yes/no target column from the income column (1 = $50k+, 0 = less than $50k). All text columns were one-hot encoded and missing values were filled in.
- **Validation:** I remade two charts from the original article to check that the dataset looks right.
- **Model:** A Random Forest model hit above 55% accuracy on the test set.


## Methods

To make sure our data is correct, I recreated two charts from the original FiveThirtyEight article. If they look similar, we can trust the data.

**Chart 1 — Films seen (among Star Wars fans)**

```{python}
# Keep only respondents who have seen at least one Star Wars film
fans = df.filter(pl.col("seen_any") == "Yes")
n_fans = len(fans)

seen_cols = [c for c in df.columns if c.startswith("seen_") and c != "seen_any"]

# Count non-null values per seen column (non-null = "Yes, they saw it")
seen_counts = pl.DataFrame({
    "film_key": seen_cols,
    "n_seen": [fans[c].drop_nulls().len() for c in seen_cols]
}).with_columns(
    (pl.col("n_seen") / n_fans * 100).alias("pct_seen"),
    pl.col("film_key")
      .str.replace("seen_", "")
      .str.replace_all("_", " ")
      .alias("film")
).sort("film_key")

(
    ggplot(seen_counts, aes(x="pct_seen", y="film"))
    + geom_bar(stat="identity", fill="#4393c3")
    + labs(
        title="Which Star Wars films have you seen?",
        subtitle="% of fans (respondents who have seen any film)",
        x="% of respondents",
        y=""
    )
    + theme_minimal()
)
```

**Chart 2 — Average film ranking (1 = best, 6 = worst)**

```{python}
rank_cols = [c for c in df.columns if c.startswith("rank_")]

rank_avgs = pl.DataFrame({
    "film_key": rank_cols,
    "avg_rank": [
        df.select(
            pl.col(c).cast(pl.Float64, strict=False)
        ).mean().item()
        for c in rank_cols
    ]
}).with_columns(
    pl.col("film_key")
      .str.replace("rank_", "")
      .str.replace_all("_", " ")
      .alias("film")
).sort("avg_rank")

(
    ggplot(rank_avgs, aes(x="avg_rank", y="film"))
    + geom_bar(stat="identity", fill="#d6604d")
    + labs(
        title="Average film ranking",
        subtitle="Lower score = ranked more favorably (1 = best, 6 = worst)",
        x="Average rank",
        y=""
    )
    + theme_minimal()
)
```

```{python}
# ── Step 1: Identify and drop non-feature columns ──────────────────────────
drop_cols = [
    c for c in df.columns
    if any(kw in c.lower() for kw in ["respondentid", "first_name", "last_name", "email"])
]
print("Columns dropped (ID / name / email):", drop_cols)
```

```{python}
# ── Step 2: Create binary target from household_income ─────────────────────
income_50k_vals = ["$50,000 - $99,999", "$100,000 - $149,999", "$150,000+"]

df_ml = (
    df.drop(drop_cols)
      .filter(pl.col("household_income").is_not_null())   # drop rows with no income label
      .with_columns(
          pl.when(pl.col("household_income").is_in(income_50k_vals))
            .then(pl.lit(1))
            .otherwise(pl.lit(0))
            .alias("target")
      )
)

print("Target distribution:")
print(df_ml["target"].value_counts().sort("target"))
```

```{python}
# ── Step 3: Separate features and target; drop original income column ───────
# household_income is now encoded in `target` — keeping it as a feature
# would make the prediction trivial, so we remove it.
target = df_ml["target"].to_numpy()
feature_df = df_ml.drop(["household_income", "target"])

# Identify column types for the ColumnTransformer
numeric_cols = feature_df.select(cs.numeric()).columns
cat_cols     = feature_df.select(cs.string()).columns

print(f"Numeric features : {len(numeric_cols)}")
print(f"Categorical features: {len(cat_cols)}")
print(f"Total rows after filtering: {len(feature_df)}")
```

```{python}
# ── Step 4: Convert to pandas (via dict — no pyarrow required) 
# sklearn requires pandas DataFrames or numpy arrays; pyarrow is not installed,
# so we use a dict-of-lists conversion which handles nulls as None → NaN.
X = pd.DataFrame({col: feature_df[col].to_list() for col in feature_df.columns})
```

- **Number columns** — filled in with the median of that column.
- **Text columns** — filled in with the most common value in that column.


## Results

```{python}
# ── Build preprocessing + classification pipeline
numeric_transformer = Pipeline([
    ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="median"))
])

categorical_transformer = Pipeline([
    ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="most_frequent")),
    ("onehot", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore", sparse_output=False))
])

preprocessor = ColumnTransformer([
    ("num", numeric_transformer, numeric_cols),
    ("cat", categorical_transformer, cat_cols)
])

pipeline = Pipeline([
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("classifier", RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42))
])
```

```{python}
# ── Train / test split and model fitting ───────────────────────────────────
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, target, test_size=0.2, random_state=42, stratify=target
)

pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred = pipeline.predict(X_test)
```

```{python}
# ── Accuracy and classification report ────────────────────────────────────
accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
print(f"Test Accuracy: {accuracy:.4f}  ({accuracy * 100:.1f}%)\n")
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred, target_names=["< $50k", ">= $50k"]))
```

```{python}
# ── Feature importance (top 20) ───────────────────────────────────────────
rf_model = pipeline.named_steps["classifier"]
ohe = pipeline.named_steps["preprocessor"].named_transformers_["cat"].named_steps["onehot"]

# Reconstruct feature names after one-hot encoding
cat_feature_names = ohe.get_feature_names_out(cat_cols).tolist()
all_feature_names = list(numeric_cols) + cat_feature_names

importance_df = pl.DataFrame({
    "feature": all_feature_names,
    "importance": rf_model.feature_importances_.tolist()
}).sort("importance", descending=True).head(20)

(
    ggplot(importance_df, aes(x="importance", y="feature"))
    + geom_bar(stat="identity", fill="#2ca02c")
    + labs(
        title="Top 20 Feature Importances",
        subtitle="Random Forest — predicting household income ≥ $50k",
        x="Mean decrease in impurity",
        y=""
    )
    + theme_minimal()
)
```


## Conclusion

The Random Forest model got above 55% accuracy, which meets the project goal. The most useful features for predicting income were things like age and education, which makes sense. Star Wars opinions didn't matter as much, but they did help a little.

A few things to keep in mind though:

- The survey is from 2014, so the data is pretty old and income patterns have probably changed.
- Some people didn't answer the income question, so I had to remove those rows. If lower-income people were more likely to skip it, that could skew the model.
- The model could probably be improved with more data or by tuning the settings.

Overall this was a good exercise in cleaning messy survey data and building a full ML pipeline from scratch.
